title: Miami Vice Drive, Vol. 2: Vapor Coast — Last Season
tonic_progression: Cm (Aeolian, the marina at first light — picking up exactly where Vol. 1 closed, but the pastel has curdled and the season has turned) -> Gm (Aeolian, NOT the Dorian cruise of Vol. 1's track 2 — the cruise locks in already heavy, the brightness gone) -> Dm (Aeolian, the barometer drops, first storm light over the bay) -> Am (Phrygian color, the empire's books no longer balance) -> Em (Phrygian, the wire goes live, the net tightens) -> Bm (Phrygian, the hurricane bands arrive, the last clean run impossible) -> F#m (Phrygian/Locrian-leaning b5 menace, THE DARK APEX — the raid/storm hits at once, the most dissonant harmony of either volume, NO release) -> C#m (Aeolian, the aftermath, sirens and rain on emptied causeways) -> G#m / Abm (Aeolian, sinking through the flooded underworld, the deepest tonal center) -> Ebm / D#m (Aeolian, the safe houses dark, everything washed out to sea) -> Bbm (Aeolian, the comedown's tender floor, the empire is ash) -> Cm (Aeolian, back at the marina as the storm clears to a grey dawn — the ring closes minor, the engine cold, no sun comes up)
narrative_arc: Twelve tracks read as the empire's last nights — late-'87 hurricane season folding the pastel dream into dread, minor throughout, with NO major-key release anywhere: the comedown Vol. 1's glamour earned, paid in full. The album picks up EXACTLY where Vol. 1 closed — the chrome Daytona at the marina before dawn — but the season has turned and the pastel has curdled. Tracks 1-3 are the turning of the season: the marina at first light, the same tonic home (Cm) Vol. 1 ended on but the air now heavy and the sky bruising, the VICE ARP returning slow and filtered as in Vol. 1's opener but DARKENED — the same six notes, now reharmonised under a storm-grey pad (1, Cm Aeolian, the only drumless track); the cruise locks in already heavy, NOT the Dorian brightness of Vol. 1 but flat Aeolian, the chrome of the car reading dull under a low sky (2, Gm Aeolian); and the barometer drops, the first storm light moving over the bay as the deal-flow tightens (3, Dm Aeolian, the PALM-SHADOW PULSE returning but stripped of its glide, dragging). Tracks 4-7 are the net tightening and the storm gathering — the tension arc with no pastel sheen left to hide under: the empire's books no longer balance and the SAX-NOIR LEAD returns curdled, lower and more haunted than Vol. 1 (4, Am Phrygian); the wire goes live, the net tightening as Phrygian b2 menace becomes the album's default rather than a single peak color (5, Em Phrygian); the first hurricane bands arrive and the last clean run becomes impossible, the pursuit logic of Vol. 1's track 6 now a storm-cornered flight (6, Bm Phrygian); and track 7 is THE DARK APEX — the raid and the storm hitting at once, the album's peak BPM and most dissonant harmony (F#m with a Locrian-leaning b5), the VICE ARP weaponised as in Vol. 1's run but now SHATTERING rather than chasing, the SAX-NOIR LEAD screaming into static — and CRUCIALLY there is no release on the far side, no major-key burst out onto open water; where Vol. 1 broke clean into A major here the apex collapses straight into the aftermath (7, F#m Phrygian/Locrian, BPM ceiling 112). Tracks 8-12 are the long comedown through the drowned city — the album's structural weight, the empire's ash: the aftermath, sirens and rain on emptied causeways, the brightness that never comes (8, C#m Aeolian); sinking through the flooded underworld, the safe houses going dark (9, G#m Aeolian); everything washed out to sea, the network gone, the SAX-NOIR LEAD alone and drowning (10, Ebm Aeolian); the comedown's tender floor, the empire reduced to ash and salt-water, the most tender and lowest point of either volume (11, Bbm Aeolian); and the ring closes back at the marina as the storm clears to a grey dawn — the same Cm the album opened on and that BOTH volumes opened/closed on, the VICE ARP played whole one final time but COLD, the engine that ticked-as-it-cooled in Vol. 1 now simply cold, no sun coming up, the season and the empire both over (12, Cm Aeolian, the ring closed minor, no payoff). The listener arrives not at false dawn (Vol. 1) but at a grey washed-out morning after — the glamour spent, the dread fully paid.

recurring_motifs.motif: Vice arp (CURDLED) — the same pulsing six-note ascending-then-folding arpeggio voiced on a glassy DX7-style FM electric piano carried forward from Vol. 1 (root-5-b3-octave-5-b3, the Jan-Hammer-ADJACENT pulsing sixteenth feel, its own contour, never a copy), but DARKENED for the last season: reharmonised under storm-grey pads, the glassy sheen filtered down toward a duller, salt-corroded timbre, the cruise pulse that drove Vol. 1's neon nights now reading as the nervous tick of an empire running out of time. It is the two-volume album-frame motif — it opened Vol. 1 and closed Vol. 1, and it opens Vol. 2 and closes Vol. 2.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 reintroduces it SLOW AND FILTERED exactly as Vol. 1's opener did — the glassy FM electric piano alone, no rhythm, the six notes spread wide — but reharmonised under a storm-grey pad so the SAME motif now reads heavy and bruised rather than anticipatory (the deliberate audible signal that this is Vol. 1's world, curdled). Track 4 plays it FULL AND PULSING under the curdled sax lead as the empire's books stop balancing — the cruise engine of Vol. 1's deal-arc returning, but the sheen filtered down and the harmony Phrygian-leaning, the pulse now anxious rather than confident. Track 7 (the dark apex) plays it WEAPONISED-AND-SHATTERING — chopped into a relentless 16th-note ostinato as in Vol. 1's run, but bit-crushed and ring-modulated at the edges and dragged against the Locrian b5, the cruise-pulse-turned-chase-engine of Vol. 1 now a chase-engine COMING APART as the raid and storm hit at once. Track 12 plays it WHOLE AGAIN but COLD — slow, one octave down on FM electric piano as in Vol. 1's closer, but the warm pad sustain of Vol. 1 replaced by a thin grey wash and the engine that 'ticked as it cooled' in Vol. 1 now simply COLD, the same six notes remembered at a washed-out grey dawn with the empire and the season both over.

recurring_motifs.motif: Sax-noir lead (HAUNTED) — the long-breathed four-phrase melodic line voiced on a sax-flavored analog lead carried forward from Vol. 1 (breathy filter envelope, slow portamento between phrase peaks, NOT a literal sample of any soundtrack theme), the criminal-underworld-tension signature, but for Vol. 2 it is lower, more haunted and more exhausted from its first entrance — where in Vol. 1 it nosed in as fresh tension and later bloomed resolved into major, here it never gets a major to bloom into. Still withheld from the opening tracks so its first Vol. 2 entrance marks the moment the empire's books stop balancing.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Heard NOWHERE in tracks 1-3 (the season's turn is pad-and-arp dread only; the sax is held back exactly as in Vol. 1's dusk arc). Track 4 reintroduces it NOSING IN but LOWER AND HAUNTED — entering a register below its Vol. 1 entrance, breathy and frayed over the curdled vice arp at the moment the empire's books no longer balance, the criminal-underworld tension returning already tired. Track 7 (the dark apex) plays it SCREAMING INTO STATIC — pushed to the top of its register as in Vol. 1's run, but the filter opened into a harsh edge and the tail dissolving into noise as the raid and storm hit, the chase-cry of Vol. 1 now a cry going under. Track 10 plays it ALONE AND DROWNING — slow, low, unaccompanied over the Ebm pad with the portamento dragged out and a long flooded reverb, the network gone, the lead the last voice on a washed-out network (the structural inverse of Vol. 1's track-8 SOARING-resolved-into-major peak: same contour, here drowning in minor instead of blooming in major — the release Vol. 1 got that Vol. 2 is denied). Track 12 plays it for one final BREATH, faint and far behind the cold vice arp — not a farewell solo as in Vol. 1's track 11 but a single exhausted exhale almost lost in the grey wash, the tension not resolved but simply spent.

recurring_motifs.motif: Palm-shadow pulse (STORM-DRAGGED) — the four-note descending glassy-pad figure landing on the and-of-4 carried forward from Vol. 1 (offbeat anchor that pulls each bar forward), voiced on layered DX7 electric-piano pads, Vol. 1's visual-rhythm signature for palm shadows strobing across a moving hood — but for the last season the palms are bending in hurricane wind and the figure is dragged, detuned and rain-smeared, the visual-rhythm signature of a coast going under a storm.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 3 reintroduces it under the verse as in Vol. 1's track 3, but STRIPPED OF ITS GLIDE — the offbeat pad figure now dragging slightly behind the beat and lightly detuned, the palms beginning to bend as the first storm light moves over the bay. Track 6 brings it into LEAD POSITION as an extended refrain exactly as in Vol. 1's track 6, but STORM-DRIVEN rather than pursuit-driven — sidechained hard against rolling thunder-pad swells, the palms whipping flat as the first hurricane bands arrive and the last clean run becomes impossible. Track 9 returns it FLOODED — heavily filtered and pitch-drifting, the high end gone, half-submerged in a long wet reverb, the palm shadows now seen through floodwater as the listener sinks through the drowned underworld (the storm-season answer to Vol. 1's track-10 salt-fog ghosting — there the windshield was smeared, here the street is underwater). Track 11 returns it as a single SLOW DETUNED FRAGMENT, just two of the four notes, drifting through the tender Bbm floor like a downed palm frond on still floodwater — the motif's farewell, the visual rhythm finally stilled.

tracks.title: Season's Turn

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - storm-grey DX7 FM electric-piano pad alone, no rhythm, slow filter sweep opening over 30s, a low distant pressure groan under the pad like a barometer dropping]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + CURDLED vice arp slow and filtered (the six-note Vol. 1 figure, now reharmonised heavy and bruised under the grey pad), soft sub bass on root]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + layered grey pad swell, gated reverb snare brushed soft on 2 and 4, mellow analog bass walking root-flat-sixth (heavier than Vol. 1's root-fifth), distant low thunder texture]

sections.name: bridge
sections.instrumentation: [Bridge - drop to grey pad + curdled arp only, breathe, filter half-closed, the heavy air held]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - fade pad sustaining C, drums drop out, curdled vice arp once more then near-silence on G (V of Cm)]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 88 BPM C minor Aeolian, storm-grey DX7 FM electric-piano pad, slow filtered pulsing six-note arpeggio reharmonised heavy and bruised, soft gated reverb drums entering at 1:00, mellow analog sub bass walking root and flat sixth, distant low thunder texture and a dropping-barometer pressure groan, late-1987 Miami hurricane season turning, the pastel dream curdled into dread, nocturnal and bruised and foreboding, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a chrome Ferrari Daytona at a Miami marina at heavy grey first light, the bay flat and pewter-grey, pastel deco hotels behind gone dull and overcast, a bruised storm sky the colour of an old wound massing on the horizon over the water, palm silhouettes beginning to lean in a rising wind, the pastel pink and turquoise palette curdled and desaturated toward grey and slate, foreboding heavy-air mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Picks up the two-volume seam directly: Vol. 1 closed home on C minor at the marina before dawn (the vice arp re-read as a wind-down). Vol. 2 opens on the SAME C minor at the SAME marina — but the pastel has curdled and the season has turned, the same tonic home re-cast from false-dawn rest into heavy-air dread. The deliberate identical tonic across the volume seam is the audible signal that this is Vol. 1's world, after the glamour.
tracks.composer_notes: OPENER / VOLUME SEAM PICKUP. Re-establishes the two-volume tonal home (C minor) and the signature voice — the glassy DX7 FM electric-piano pad — but CURDLED: the SAME vice arp that opened Vol. 1 slow-and-filtered returns slow-and-filtered here, deliberately recognisable, but reharmonised heavy and bruised under a storm-grey pad so the listener feels Vol. 1's world after the dream has soured. NO drums until bar 32. This is the album's only drumless track. Establishes the storm-season dread that replaces Vol. 1's pastel-bright surface — minor throughout, NO brightness anywhere, and there will be no major-key payoff at any point in this volume. Outro ends on G (V of C minor), reread as G minor tonic for track 2 (parallel-minor pivot, same trick as Vol. 1). Do not let ACE-Step add a hi-hat shuffle or brighten this — keep it grey and heavy.

tracks.title: Low Sky Cruise

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - grey pad in Gm flat-Aeolian, sidechain hint, a low ignition swell with no spark to it]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + pulsing vice arp on root-flat-third-fifth (Aeolian, NOT the raised-sixth Dorian of Vol. 1's track 2), soft kick on every beat]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + mellow analog lead on a minor phrase that never lifts, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, sub bass]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - drop lead, layer second grey pad an octave up, no brightening]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full kit + lead doubled in octaves, sidechain locks in heavy]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter sweep down, pad sustains G then resolves to D (V of Gm), primes the heavier cruise]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 92 BPM G minor Aeolian, heavy overcast analog pad, sidechained pulsing arpeggio on glassy FM electric piano in flat Aeolian, soft kick on every beat, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, a mellow analog lead on a minor phrase that never lifts, a chrome Ferrari Daytona threading a causeway under a low overcast sky, the chrome dull and the night joyless, steady heavy nocturnal cruise with no brightness, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a chrome Ferrari Daytona on a causeway lined with pastel deco hotels under a low heavy overcast sky, the neon signage dim and half-lit against the grey, the chrome of the car gone dull, palm trees leaning in a steady wind, wet pavement reflecting a colourless sky, the pastel pink and turquoise palette muted hard toward slate and overcast grey, joyless heavy mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Cm to Gm via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 1's G outro reread as G minor tonic) — the same cycle-of-fifths-descending engine as Vol. 1. BUT where Vol. 1's track 2 brightened Gm to DORIAN (raised 6, E natural) for the cruise's pastel lift, Vol. 2 keeps Gm flat AEOLIAN — the deliberate audible darkening of the same key, the cruise that locked in bright in Vol. 1 now locking in heavy.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +4 BPM. First step of the linear ascent (the same +4 ramp shape as Vol. 1, but the BPM floor and ceiling are each shifted DOWN two ticks: 88->112 here vs Vol. 1's 90->114, the last season slightly slower and heavier throughout).
tracks.composer_notes: First cruiser. The pulsing vice arp ignites as the cruise engine as in Vol. 1's track 2 — sidechain obvious but tasteful — but voiced flat AEOLIAN (root-b3-5, NO raised sixth) so the SAME ignition that brightened Vol. 1 here reads heavy and joyless. This single mode swap (Dorian->Aeolian on the same key) is the album's first structural statement that there is no pastel brightness left. Keep the lead's phrase resolutely minor — it must never imply a lift. Outro ends on D (V of Gm), reread as D minor tonic of track 3 — earned pivot.

tracks.title: Barometer Falling

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - sub bass on D, kick on every beat, distant low pressure groan deepening]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + STORM-DRAGGED palm-shadow pulse motif (4-note descending pad on and-of-4, now stripped of its glide, dragging slightly behind the beat and lightly detuned), gated snare]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + mellow analog lead, layered grey pads, sidechain, first storm-light texture (distant filtered thunder swells)]

sections.name: breakdown
sections.instrumentation: [Breakdown - kick drops, just the dragged palm-shadow pulse + grey pad + long reverb tail, the storm light moving over the bay]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full kit returns, lead doubled, heavier]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close, pad on A (V of Dm), thunder texture rolling]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 96 BPM D minor Aeolian, four-note descending pad figure on the offbeat dragging slightly behind the beat and lightly detuned, sidechained grey DX7 pad, mellow analog lead, gated reverb drums, kick on every beat, analog sub bass, distant filtered thunder swells and a deepening pressure groan, the barometer falling as the first storm light moves over the bay, dragging uneasy nocturnal cruise, the empire's deal-flow tightening, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a coastal Miami boulevard at night with the first storm light flickering green-grey on the horizon over the bay, pastel deco storefronts dim, a chrome Ferrari Daytona in motion mid-frame, palm trees bending harder in a freshening wind, the first fat raindrops streaking the air, wet asphalt reflecting a sickly storm-grey sky, the pastel pink and turquoise palette saturated dark toward storm-grey and bruised green, uneasy storm-light mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Gm to Dm via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 2's D outro reread as D minor tonic). Continuing the cycle-of-fifths-descending ladder strictly inside the minor mode, flat Aeolian throughout (NO Dorian color anywhere in Vol. 2's cruise tracks, unlike Vol. 1).
tracks.composer_notes: FIRST RETURN of the palm-shadow pulse motif, as in Vol. 1's track 3 (under the verse as support, not yet lead) — but STORM-DRAGGED: the offbeat figure now drags slightly behind the beat and is lightly detuned, the palms beginning to bend, the glide of Vol. 1 gone. The breakdown at bar 41 is where the listener should first hear the dragged motif unobstructed. The first storm-light textures (distant filtered thunder) enter here — the visual dread arriving before the harmonic dread peaks. Outro A (V of Dm) pivots cleanly into Am of track 4.

tracks.title: The Books Won't Balance

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - filtered curdled vice arp in Am with a Phrygian b2 (Bb) shadow, building, faint pad unease]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + kick on every beat, sub bass, gated snare]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + HAUNTED sax-noir lead NOSING IN low (a register below Vol. 1's entrance) and frayed over the curdled arp, layered grey pads]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - sax lead drops, CURDLED vice arp returns full and pulsing to foreground, Phrygian b2 shadow held]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full mix, haunted sax lead returns, sidechain harder]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - sax lead holds E (V of Am), filter close]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 100 BPM A minor with a Phrygian flat-second shadow, full pulsing glassy arpeggio reharmonised dark, hard kick on every beat, gated reverb snare, a breathy frayed sax-flavored analog lead nosing in low and haunted over the arp, sidechained layered grey DX7 pads, the coke empire's books no longer balancing, criminal-underworld tension returning already tired under a storm-grey sky, haunted noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a dim back office above a deco storefront at night seen through a rain-streaked window, a desk lamp and an open ledger glowing sickly amber, pastel pink and turquoise neon bleeding dimly through the wet glass from the street below, a chrome Ferrari Daytona parked at the curb in the rain outside, a storm sky pressing low over the rooftops, the palette curdled toward bruised amber and storm-grey, haunted tense noir mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Dm to Am via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 3's A outro reread as A minor tonic). Where Vol. 1's track 4 brightened Am to DORIAN (raised 6, F#) for the deal-arc's pastel sheen, Vol. 2 darkens Am toward PHRYGIAN (flat-second shadow, Bb) — the menace that Vol. 1 saved for its single track-6 peak is here brought forward and made the album's default modal color, the tension arc with no brightness to hide under.
tracks.composer_notes: SAX-NOIR LEAD REINTRODUCED — nosing in as in Vol. 1's track 4, but LOWER AND HAUNTED (a register below its Vol. 1 entrance), already tired, never given the fresh-tension brightness it had in Vol. 1. The CURDLED VICE ARP returns full and pulsing under it, its second structural appearance. The Phrygian b2 shadow (Bb over Am) is the audible signal that Vol. 2 runs darker than Vol. 1 from the deal-arc onward. Keep the sax breathy and restrained here (it screams into static later in track 7). Outro E (V of Am), reread as E minor tonic of track 5 — earned pivot.

tracks.title: The Wire Goes Live

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - filtered arp in Em Phrygian (F natural b2), pad filter sweeps implying surveillance and distant sirens, building]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + hard kick on every beat, sub bass, gated snare]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + aggressive analog lead, layered grey pads, FM bell counter-melody cold and brittle]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - lead drops, curdled vice arp returns to foreground, the net tightening]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full mix, sidechain harder, harmony tightening, b2 menace pronounced]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - lead holds B (V of Em), filter close]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 104 BPM E minor Phrygian (flat second, F natural), driving pulsing arpeggio, hard kick on every beat, gated reverb snare, an aggressive analog lead, a cold brittle FM bell counter-melody, pad filter sweeps implying surveillance and distant sirens, sidechained layered grey DX7 pads, the wire going live as the net tightens around the empire, Phrygian menace as the album's default, tense and menacing storm-noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a deserted neon-lit Miami side street at night in the rain, an unmarked sedan with a faint interior glow parked in shadow across from a deco building, pastel storefront signage flickering pink and turquoise through the downpour, a chrome Ferrari Daytona pulling away with taillights smearing on wet asphalt, distant blue-and-red light glow refracted in the rain, the palette curdled toward wet slate and bruised neon, tense menacing surveillance mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Am to Em via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 4's E outro reread as E minor tonic). The Phrygian b2 (F natural over Em) is now explicit and foregrounded — where Vol. 1 reached Phrygian only at its single track-6 peak, Vol. 2 has been Phrygian-leaning since track 4 and is now fully Phrygian, the menace sustained rather than spiked.
tracks.composer_notes: TENSION ARC. The net tightens; the harmony is now explicitly Phrygian (b2 = F natural). The pad's filter sweeps imply surveillance and distant sirens without literal sound-effects. The cold brittle FM bell counter-melody foreshadows the FM-bell voice that carries the cold vice arp in track 12 (same structural role as Vol. 1's track-5 FM bell, but brittle and cold rather than warm). Em is the gravity well between the haunted Am of track 4 and the storm-cornered Bm of track 6. Outro B (V of Em), reread as B minor tonic of track 6 — earned pivot.

tracks.title: Hurricane Bands

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - pad swell + reverse cymbal up to bar 1, Phrygian b2 (C natural) color, rolling thunder-pad swells beneath]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - kick on every beat, sub bass, pulsing arp with Phrygian b2 edge]

sections.name: pre-chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Pre-chorus - build, snare rolls, riser, filter open, wind-texture rising]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - STORM-DRAGGED palm-shadow pulse motif in lead position, extended 8-bar refrain, sidechained hard against rolling thunder-pad swells, layered grey pads, the palms whipping flat]

sections.name: break
sections.instrumentation: [Break - drums drop, just storm-dragged palm-shadow pulse + grey pad + howling wind texture, the last clean run becoming impossible]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full return, double-octave lead over the pulse, the storm closing in]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on F# (V of Bm) holding into the apex, wind rising]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 108 BPM B minor Phrygian, four-note descending offbeat pulse motif in the lead voice storm-driven and detuned, pulsing arpeggio with a Phrygian flat-second edge, wide analog lead, layered atmospheric grey DX7 pads, gated reverb drums, hard kick, sub bass, rolling thunder-pad swells and a rising howling wind texture, reverse cymbal swells, the first hurricane bands arriving as the last clean run becomes impossible, menacing storm-driven cornered noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a grid of palm-lined Miami side streets seen from above at night in a hurricane outer band, palm trees whipped flat by violent wind, sheets of rain slanting across the streets, a single chrome Ferrari Daytona weaving through the flooding roads with neon reflecting in the running water, pastel pink and turquoise streetlamps swinging in the wind, the palette curdled toward storm-grey, bruised green and dark slate, cornered storm-driven menacing mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Em to Bm via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 5's B outro reread as B minor tonic). Phrygian b2 (C natural over Bm) — the same key and mode Vol. 1 used for its single track-6 pursuit peak, here re-cast as a storm-cornered flight rather than a clean pursuit; the structural slot rhymes with Vol. 1 but the destination on the far side is catastrophe, not release.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +4 BPM. Maintaining the linear ascent into the apex (the BPM ceiling is one track away).
tracks.composer_notes: BRIDGE INTO THE APEX. The STORM-DRAGGED palm-shadow pulse moves into LEAD POSITION as an extended 8-bar refrain (its structural payoff, mirroring Vol. 1's track 6) — but storm-driven against rolling thunder-pad swells, the palms whipping flat as the first hurricane bands arrive. 270s (45s longer) — the bridge into the catastrophe deserves the extra room, as Vol. 1's track 6 did. The break at bar 41 (pulse on solo pad + howling wind) is the held breath before the apex. STRUCTURAL NOTE for the pipeline: this rhymes with Vol. 1's track-6 bridge-into-peak slot, but where Vol. 1 broke from here into a major-key release, Vol. 2 breaks into a DISSONANT minor apex (track 7) and stays minor — there is NO major payoff anywhere in this volume. Outro F# (V of Bm), reread as F# minor tonic of track 7 — earned pivot.

tracks.title: Eye Wall

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - kick + sub bass + sidechain grey pad, no melody yet, the raid and storm igniting at once, Locrian b5 (C natural over F#m) shadow entering]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + WEAPONISED-AND-SHATTERING vice arp as relentless 16th-note ostinato, bit-crushed and ring-modulated at the edges, dragged against the Locrian b5, gated snare, layered grey pads]

sections.name: pre-chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Pre-chorus - build, white-noise riser merging into storm-wind roar, snare roll]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - SCREAMING-INTO-STATIC sax-noir lead at the top of its register, filter opened to a harsh edge, tail dissolving into noise, over the shattering ostinato, hard sidechain]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse2 - drop the sax lead, shattering arp ostinato carries alone against the storm roar]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - full mix, sax lead doubled an octave up screaming, the catastrophe at its loudest]

sections.name: bridge
sections.instrumentation: [Bridge - drums lurch and stutter, shattering arp ostinato fragmenting, the eye wall passing over]

sections.name: chorus3
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus3 - full return, the apex held at maximum dissonance]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - everything collapses, sax lead and arp cut to a ringing C# (V of F#m), no resolution, drums fall away into rain]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 112 BPM F-sharp minor Phrygian with a Locrian flat-fifth shadow, relentless 16th-note arpeggio ostinato on glassy FM electric piano bit-crushed and ring-modulated at the edges, hard kick on every beat, a screaming sax-flavored analog lead at the top of its register with the filter opened to a harsh edge and the tail dissolving into noise, gated reverb drums lurching and stuttering, sidechained layered grey pads, white-noise risers merging into a storm-wind roar, the police raid and the hurricane eye wall hitting at once, catastrophic shattering dissonant zenith with no release, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, the hurricane eye wall hitting the Miami coast at night, palm trees torn sideways, neon signs ripped loose and sparking, a chrome Ferrari Daytona stopped at a flooded intersection in the full fury of the storm with floodwater rising over its wheels, blue-and-red emergency light strobing through sheets of rain, the pastel pink and turquoise palette violently fractured into storm-black, bruised green and electric blue-white, catastrophic shattering apex mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.cover_notes_internal: This is the album's catastrophe-hero cover — the most violent and dramatic image of either volume, the storm-and-raid apex; it should read as the dark inverse of Vol. 1's bright track-7/8 hero covers on the playlist thumbnail grid.
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Bm to F#m via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 6's F# outro reread as F# minor tonic) — the SAME key Vol. 1 used for its track-7 run/peak, here re-cast as the album's most dissonant harmony with a Locrian-leaning b5 (C natural over F#m). CRITICALLY: where Vol. 1's track 7 ended on C# and lifted by chromatic-mediant into A MAJOR (the bright release), Vol. 2's track 7 ends on a ringing UNRESOLVED C# that collapses straight into the C#-MINOR aftermath of track 8 — the major release Vol. 1 earned is deliberately denied here. This is the structural heart of Vol. 2's no-major-payoff thesis.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +4 BPM. The album's BPM ceiling (112, validation-safe under 118; two ticks below Vol. 1's 114 ceiling — the last season slightly slower even at its catastrophe, the power in dread and weight, not speed).
tracks.composer_notes: THE DARK APEX — peak BPM, most dissonant track of either volume. The VICE ARP is WEAPONISED-AND-SHATTERING (Vol. 1's weaponised run-ostinato, now bit-crushed/ring-modulated and dragged against the Locrian b5 — the chase-engine coming apart). The SAX-NOIR LEAD SCREAMS INTO STATIC (Vol. 1's run-cry, now dissolving into noise). 300s is intentional — the catastrophe should feel like it lasts a full night. The bridge at bar 65 (stuttering, fragmenting) is the eye wall passing over. CRITICAL NO-PAYOFF DESIGN: the outro does NOT resolve and does NOT lift into major — it collapses on a ringing unresolved C# that falls straight into the C#-MINOR aftermath of track 8. This is the deliberate inverse of Vol. 1's F#m-to-A-major lift: same 7->8 seam pitch (C#), but here C# stays the dominant of a MINOR aftermath instead of becoming the major third of a bright release. FLAG FOR HUMAN REVIEW: the collapse-into-minor at the 7->8 seam is the album's thesis made audible; if the render lifts or brightens it at the seam, re-cut the outro darker — there must be NO sense of release on the far side of the apex. Do not let ACE-Step add a triumphant chord or a major resolution anywhere in this track.

sections.name: chorus

sections.name: chorus2

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on G# (V of C#m), pad fade, rain continuing]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 104 BPM C-sharp minor, dim pulsing arpeggio, an exhausted minor analog lead, brushed gated snare, soft sidechained grey DX7 pads, sub bass, heavy rain texture and distant receding sirens in the pad filter, the aftermath of the raid and the storm on emptied rain-soaked causeways, no relief and no brightness arriving, exhausted aftermath noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, an emptied Miami causeway in the rain just after the storm's first pass, abandoned cars and downed palm fronds across flooded lanes, a chrome Ferrari Daytona stopped at an angle with hazard lights blinking, distant emergency lights pulsing blue-and-red through the rain far down the causeway, the bay churning grey on both sides, the pastel pink and turquoise palette drowned toward wet grey and bruised dark, exhausted rain-soaked aftermath mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: The apex's ringing unresolved C# (V of F#m) is reread DOWN as the C# MINOR tonic of the aftermath — a deceptive collapse rather than a resolution. This is the deliberate inverse of Vol. 1's 7->8 move: Vol. 1 turned C# into the major third of a bright A-major release; Vol. 2 keeps C# minor as the tonic of an exhausted aftermath. The brightness Vol. 1 got at this exact structural seam is denied; the album drops into its long minor comedown instead of a major peak.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -8 BPM. The deceleration begins immediately at the aftermath — a hard drop (steeper than Vol. 1's -4, because there is no major-peak plateau to ease through; the album falls straight into comedown). The BPM drops as the energy collapses rather than releasing.
tracks.composer_notes: COMEDOWN BEGINS / the no-payoff hinge. This is the structural slot where Vol. 1 burst into its A-major peak — here it is deliberately the EXHAUSTED MINOR AFTERMATH instead, the album's thesis: the comedown Vol. 1's glamour earned. The apex collapses into C# minor with NO brightness, NO release, NO major chord. The sax-noir lead does NOT return here (it gets its drowning solo in track 10) — this track belongs to the spent pad-and-arp and the rain. Keep it exhausted, rain-soaked, minor; do not let ACE-Step brighten the post-apex track into relief. Outro G# (V of C#m), reread as G# minor tonic of track 9 — the descent into the drowned underworld continues.

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 98 BPM G-sharp minor, four-note descending offbeat pulse motif heavily filtered and pitch-drifting with the high end gone, half-submerged in long wet reverb, mellow analog lead, layered grey pads, soft kick, gated reverb snare, sub bass, water-lapping texture, sinking through a flooded Miami underworld as the safe houses go dark, submerged tired low-light noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a flooded underground parking garage or basement nightclub half-submerged in dark floodwater at night, pastel pink and turquoise neon signs glowing dimly under the water's surface, a chrome Ferrari Daytona abandoned axle-deep in the black flood, reflections of dead neon rippling on the still floodwater, the palette drowned toward dark teal, submerged neon and near-black, sinking submerged low-light mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from C#m to G#m via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 8's G# outro reread as G# minor tonic). G# minor is the album's deepest tonal center so far — the descent into the flooded underworld continuing strictly inside the minor mode, the comedown sinking rather than easing.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -6 BPM. The comedown deepens its descent into the drowned city.
tracks.composer_notes: COMEDOWN DEEPENS. The PALM-SHADOW PULSE returns FLOODED — heavily filtered, pitch-drifting, half-submerged in wet reverb, the palm shadows now seen through floodwater (the storm-season answer to Vol. 1's track-10 salt-fog ghosting; there the windshield was smeared, here the street is underwater). Keep it sinking and tired. Outro D# (V of G#m), reread as Eb minor tonic of track 10 (D#=Eb enharmonic) — the descent reaches its deepest, everything washed out to sea.

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 94 BPM E-flat minor, warm-grey analog pad, a sax-flavored analog lead alone and drowning with slow dragged-out portamento and a long flooded reverb, dim wet glassy plucked counter-melody, soft kick, gated reverb snare brushed light, sub bass, deep flooded reverb, everything washed out to sea as the network goes dark, the last voice on a dead network, drowning desolate spent noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a desolate storm-wrecked Miami shoreline at the bleak hour after the storm, a chrome Ferrari Daytona half-buried in wet sand at the tideline with the grey churning sea reaching its doors, scattered debris and a single dead neon sign washed up on the beach, a colourless storm-grey sky meeting a grey sea with no horizon line, the pastel pink and turquoise palette washed out almost entirely to grey, drowning desolate spent mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from G#m to Ebm via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 9's D#=Eb outro reread as Eb minor tonic). Eb minor is the album's lowest and most desolate tonal space yet — the comedown approaching its floor, everything washed out to sea.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -4 BPM. The comedown approaches its tender floor.
tracks.composer_notes: THE DROWNING. The SAX-NOIR LEAD returns ALONE AND DROWNING — slow, low, unaccompanied over the Ebm pad with the portamento dragged out and a long flooded reverb, the last voice on a dead network. This is the deliberate STRUCTURAL INVERSE of Vol. 1's track-8 SOARING-resolved-into-major peak: the same melodic contour, here drowning in minor instead of blooming in major — the release Vol. 1 was given that Vol. 2 is denied, placed at a rhyming late-album slot to make the denial felt. The break at bar 33 (sax alone in flooded reverb) is the album's emotional gut-punch. Keep the mix desolate and spent. Outro Bb (V of Ebm), reread as Bb minor tonic of track 11 — the comedown's tender floor.

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 90 BPM B-flat minor, warm-but-grey analog pad, a tender minor analog lead, glassy plucked counter-melody, a single slow detuned four-note offbeat pulse fragment drifting through, soft kick, gated reverb snare brushed very light, sub bass, deep reverb, the empire reduced to ash and salt-water in the bleak hour before a grey dawn, tender elegiac spent storm-noir, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a burnt-out deco mansion at the waterline smouldering faintly in the grey hour before dawn after the storm, wet ash and downed palms across a flooded lawn, a chrome Ferrari Daytona parked askew and rain-beaded at the curb, a single half-dead pastel neon sign flickering pink over the ruin, a sky going from storm-black to pale ashen grey at the horizon with no colour in it, the pastel pink and turquoise palette reduced to ash-grey with one dying ember of neon, tender elegiac spent mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Ebm to Bbm via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot (track 10's Bb outro reread as Bb minor tonic). Bb minor is the album's tender floor — the most tender and lowest emotional point of either volume, the empire reduced to ash. (Bb minor is also the relative minor of Vol. 1's home-adjacent Db; here it is the comedown's grieving floor, not a brightening.)
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -4 BPM. The comedown reaches its tender floor.
tracks.composer_notes: COMEDOWN'S TENDER FLOOR — the most tender and lowest point of either volume. The PALM-SHADOW PULSE makes its farewell here as a single SLOW DETUNED FRAGMENT (just two of the four notes) drifting through like a downed palm frond on still floodwater — the visual rhythm finally stilled. The sax-noir lead does NOT solo here (it had its drowning in track 10) — this track is the tender minor lead and the spent pad, the empire's elegy. Keep it tender, NOT dragging; the album is winding to its close. Outro F (V of Bbm) resolves toward C (the dominant of the home Cm) to prime the ring-close into track 12 — a chromatic re-aim from Bbm's dominant onto Cm's dominant region (the harmony reaching for home).
tracks.key_relationship_note_internal: The 11->12 pivot leaves the strict descending-cycle ladder to reach home: Bbm's outro is re-aimed toward C-as-dominant so track 12 can close on the C-minor two-volume home tonic. This is the one deliberate non-cycle pivot, exactly as the ring-close requires.

tracks.title: Cold Marina, Grey Dawn

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - deep grey DX7 pad in Cm alone, no rhythm, slow filter open over 30s, thin cold wash, no warmth]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + VICE ARP on FM electric piano, one octave below original, slow phrasing, COLD (thin grey wash beneath instead of Vol. 1's warm pad), sub bass on root]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + soft kick on 1, gated snare brushed very soft, mellow minor analog lead, layered grey pads, the sax-noir lead a single faint distant exhale far behind the arp]

sections.name: break
sections.instrumentation: [Break - drums drop, cold FM-electric-piano vice arp alone with thin reverb, the engine simply cold]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus2 - drums return softly, lead octave-doubled, full but quiet cold mix]

sections.name: coda
sections.instrumentation: [Coda - lead drops out, cold FM-electric-piano vice arp one final time whole, grey pad fades, drums fade, silence — no warmth, no sun, no resolution to major]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 86 BPM C minor Aeolian, deep cold grey DX7 pad, vice arpeggio on FM electric piano played slow and full one octave down with a thin grey wash beneath, soft kick on 1, brushed gated reverb snare, mellow minor analog lead, a single faint distant sax exhale far behind, sub bass on root, a chrome Daytona back at the marina as the storm clears to a colourless grey dawn, the engine cold, no sun coming up, the empire and the season both over, resigned cold nocturnal noir with no payoff, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: synthwave 1987 album cover, a chrome Ferrari Daytona back at the same quiet Miami marina as the storm clears to a flat grey dawn, the bay still and pewter-grey under a colourless overcast sky with no sunrise in it, storm-wrecked palms and debris along the dock, the pastel deco hotels behind grey and shuttered, a single dead neon sign dark, the pastel pink and turquoise palette fully drained to cold grey and slate with no colour returning, resigned cold no-payoff mood, no people, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.cover_notes_internal: This is the ring-close cover — deliberately the cold grey inverse of Vol. 1's track-12 'Marina, Before Dawn' (which had a single warm pink dock lamp and a sky going to pale dawn-grey). Here the same marina has NO warm light, NO sunrise — the grey morning after, the visual statement of the no-payoff thesis across both volumes.
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Resolves from track 11's C-aimed dominant onto the C MINOR two-volume home tonic — closing the ring. C minor opens and closes BOTH volumes (Vol. 1 opened on Cm dusk and closed on Cm false-dawn; Vol. 2 opens on Cm storm-turn and closes on Cm grey-dawn). The listener arrives at the home tonic from the far side of the empire's last season — but where Vol. 1's Cm close was a warm wind-down to rest, Vol. 2's Cm close is COLD, with no warmth and no major resolution.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -4 BPM. Lowest BPM of the volume at 86, two ticks below Vol. 1's 88 floor — the listener arrives more spent than at the end of Vol. 1, which is honest for the last season.
tracks.composer_notes: CLOSER / RING-CLOSE. The VICE ARP returns WHOLE, ONE OCTAVE DOWN, SLOW, ON FM ELECTRIC PIANO — exactly as in Vol. 1's closer, but COLD: the warm pad sustain of Vol. 1 replaced by a thin grey wash, the engine that 'ticked as it cooled' in Vol. 1 now simply cold. 300s long to give the coda the air it needs. The final 16-bar coda is the cold arp alone, then silence — do NOT let ACE-Step add a fade-up tail, a stinger, OR any major chord. The album ends in C minor where both volumes began — the ring closed at the marina, but at a colourless grey dawn with no sun coming up, the empire and the season both over. CRITICAL: there is NO major-key payoff here or anywhere in this volume — the comedown Vol. 1's glamour earned is paid in full, minor to the last bar. The sax-noir lead gives only a single faint distant exhale far behind the arp (the tension spent, not resolved) — the closer belongs to the cold cruise pulse.

album_notes.overall_form: Nocturnal-cruise ring over 12 tracks, MINOR THROUGHOUT with NO major-key payoff anywhere — the deliberate dark inverse of Vol. 1's centered dual-peak-in-major arc. The album begins at the same Miami marina where Vol. 1 ended (track 1, C minor) and closes at the same marina (track 12, C minor) — but where Vol. 1's ring closed at warm false-dawn rest, Vol. 2's ring closes at a colourless grey dawn after the storm, the empire and the season both over. Three movements track the empire's last nights through late-'87 hurricane season. THE SEASON'S TURN (tracks 1-3): the marina at heavy grey first light (1, Cm Aeolian, drumless, the curdled vice arp re-establishing the two-volume home), the cruise locking in already heavy in flat Aeolian rather than Vol. 1's pastel Dorian (2, Gm Aeolian), and the barometer falling as the first storm light moves over the bay (3, Dm Aeolian, the storm-dragged palm-shadow pulse) — BPM climbing 88 -> 92 -> 96, the pastel surface already gone grey. THE NET AND THE STORM (tracks 4-7): the empire's books stop balancing and the haunted sax-noir lead returns curdled (4, Am Phrygian-shadow), the wire goes live as Phrygian menace becomes the album's default (5, Em Phrygian), the first hurricane bands arrive and the last clean run becomes impossible (6, Bm Phrygian), and the DARK APEX hits — the raid and the storm at once, the most dissonant harmony of either volume with a Locrian-leaning b5 (7, F#m Phrygian/Locrian, BPM ceiling 112) — and CRUCIALLY there is no release on the far side: where Vol. 1 broke from this exact slot into A major, Vol. 2's apex collapses straight into minor. THE LONG COMEDOWN (tracks 8-12): the aftermath on emptied rain-soaked causeways (8, C#m, the no-payoff hinge — Vol. 1's major-peak slot re-cast as exhausted minor), sinking through the flooded underworld (9, G#m), everything washed out to sea with the sax-noir lead drowning (10, Ebm — the structural inverse of Vol. 1's soaring major peak), the empire reduced to ash at the comedown's tender floor (11, Bbm), and the ring closing cold at the marina at a grey dawn (12, Cm) — BPM falling 104 -> 98 -> 94 -> 90 -> 86, a long five-track descent that carries the album's structural weight (the audience lives in the comedown, and this album IS the comedown Vol. 1 earned). The home tonic C minor frames both volumes; the descent from apex to close is given more room than the ascent because the dread, not the run, is the point.
album_notes.key_strategy: Home tonic C MINOR — the same two-volume home as Miami Vice Vol. 1, deliberately re-used to bracket the two records as one continuous fall (Vol. 1: Cm dusk to Cm false-dawn; Vol. 2: Cm storm-turn to Cm grey-dawn). The ascent (tracks 1-7) runs the SAME strict cycle-of-fifths-DESCENDING ladder as Vol. 1 inside the minor mode: Cm -> Gm -> Dm -> Am -> Em -> Bm -> F#m, each track's V chord reread as the next track's tonic via the V-as-new-tonic parallel-minor pivot. The DECISIVE difference from Vol. 1 is the MODAL color arc: where Vol. 1 threaded Dorian BRIGHTNESS through its cruise tracks (Gm/Am Dorian, raised-6) and reached Phrygian only at its single track-6 peak, Vol. 2 is flat AEOLIAN on the cruise tracks (NO raised-sixth brightness anywhere) and slides into PHRYGIAN b2 menace from track 4 onward, deepening to a LOCRIAN-leaning b5 at the apex (track 7) — the menace Vol. 1 saved for one peak is here the album's default and intensifies. CRITICALLY, the peak does NOT break the minor-only posture: where Vol. 1 lifted into A major (chromatic-mediant up from F#m) at track 8 as a bright release, Vol. 2's apex (track 7) collapses on an unresolved C# straight into the C# MINOR aftermath — the major release is denied, and the back half continues DESCENDING through ever-deeper minor keys via the same cycle-of-fifths-down engine: F#m -> C#m -> G#m -> Ebm (D#m) -> Bbm, reaching the album's lowest, most desolate tonal centers (G#m, Ebm) on the comedown rather than a major plateau. The ring then closes: track 11's Bbm dominant is re-aimed onto C-as-dominant (the one deliberate departure from the strict descending ladder) so track 12 lands on the C-minor home. The result is a complete cycle-of-fifths descent across all 12 tracks (Cm-Gm-Dm-Am-Em-Bm-F#m-C#m-G#m-Ebm-Bbm-Cm) with NO major key at any point — the harmonic engine of an empire and a season both running out. This all-minor, no-payoff posture is Vol. 2's single tonal signature distinguishing it from Vol. 1 (which was the only prior volume to resolve its peak in major) and aligns it with the all-minor Tron and Neo-Tokyo volumes while keeping the C-minor Miami home.
album_notes.tempo_strategy: BPM range 86-112 — the SAME focused-cruise band shape as Vol. 1 but shifted DOWN two ticks at both ends (Vol. 1 was 88-114), the last season slightly slower and heavier throughout, keeping the audience (late-night coders) in a focused-dread pulse rather than a chase-rave. Ascent (tracks 1-7): 88 -> 92 -> 96 -> 100 -> 104 -> 108 -> 112 (a clean linear +4 accelerando across the first seven tracks, tension and BPM rising together to the apex, exactly mirroring Vol. 1's +4 ramp shape). The BPM ceiling 112 appears once (track 7, the dark apex), two ticks below Vol. 1's 114 ceiling and safely under the 118 validation upper bound — the catastrophe is two ticks SLOWER than Vol. 1's run because the power is in dread and weight, not speed. Comedown (tracks 8-12): 104 -> 98 -> 94 -> 90 -> 86. The deceleration begins with a HARD -8 drop at track 8 (steeper than Vol. 1's gentle -4, because there is no major-peak plateau to ease through — the album falls straight from apex into comedown), then steps down -6/-4/-4/-4 through the long descent to the album's 86 floor at the closer (two ticks below Vol. 1's 88 floor). The descent is asymmetric to the ascent — a tight linear +4 ramp up across seven tracks, then a steeper drop and a longer five-track easing down — and stops below Vol. 1's floor, signaling the listener emerges more spent than at the end of Vol. 1. The album's BPM peak (track 7) and its emotional collapse are NOT offset as in Vol. 1 (where the tempo peak handed off to a major-key emotional peak one track later); here the tempo peak IS the catastrophe and the next track is the immediate collapse — the structure of a fall, not a release. Range never crosses below 80 or above 118 (validation-safe).
album_notes.motif_strategy: Three motifs carried directly from Vol. 1 and CURDLED for the last season, each with three-or-four structural appearances, threaded so they rhyme with their Vol. 1 placements while the listener feels the darkening. (1) Vice arp (CURDLED) — the same pulsing six-note glassy FM-electric-piano arpeggio (Jan-Hammer-ADJACENT, its own contour, never a copy), the two-volume album-frame motif, here reharmonised under storm-grey pads with the sheen filtered toward a salt-corroded timbre: reintroduced SLOW AND FILTERED in track 1 exactly as Vol. 1's opener but bruised under a grey pad (the audible signal this is Vol. 1's world curdled), returned FULL AND PULSING under the curdled sax lead in track 4 (the deal-arc engine, now anxious), returned WEAPONISED-AND-SHATTERING (bit-crushed, ring-modulated, dragged against the Locrian b5) in track 7 (Vol. 1's weaponised run-ostinato now coming apart at the catastrophe), and returned WHOLE ONE OCTAVE DOWN but COLD in track 12 (Vol. 1's warm wind-down closer now a cold one — same six notes, no warmth, no sun). (2) Sax-noir lead (HAUNTED) — the long-breathed sax-flavored analog lead (NOT a sample of any soundtrack theme), the criminal-underworld-tension signature, lower and more haunted than Vol. 1 from its first entrance and NEVER given a major to bloom into: WITHHELD from tracks 1-3 (as in Vol. 1's dusk arc), introduced NOSING IN LOW AND HAUNTED in track 4, returned SCREAMING INTO STATIC at the apex in track 7 (Vol. 1's run-cry now dissolving into noise), returned ALONE AND DROWNING in minor in track 10 (the deliberate STRUCTURAL INVERSE of Vol. 1's track-8 SOARING-resolved-into-MAJOR peak — same contour, drowning instead of blooming, the denied release made felt), and a single faint distant EXHALE far behind the cold arp in track 12 (the tension spent, not resolved). (3) Palm-shadow pulse (STORM-DRAGGED) — the four-note descending offbeat glassy-pad figure, Vol. 1's visual-rhythm signature, here the palms bending in hurricane wind: reintroduced under the verse STRIPPED OF ITS GLIDE (dragging, detuned) in track 3, moved to LEAD POSITION STORM-DRIVEN against thunder-pad swells in track 6 (Vol. 1's track-6 pursuit-refrain now a storm-cornered flight), returned FLOODED (filtered, pitch-drifting, half-submerged in wet reverb) in track 9 (the storm-season answer to Vol. 1's track-10 salt-fog ghosting), and a single SLOW DETUNED FRAGMENT drifting through the tender Bbm floor in track 11 (the visual rhythm finally stilled). The three motifs' appearance points are interleaved exactly as in Vol. 1 so no two land on the same track-pair, and the album-frame motif (vice arp) brackets the record (tracks 1 and 12) the way it bracketed Vol. 1 — but every transformation is a darkening of its Vol. 1 counterpart, the through-line of the whole two-volume fall.
album_notes.tracklist_continuity: Tracks 1-10 each end on the V of the next track's tonic, then the V is reread as the new tonic (parallel-minor pivot trick), running a COMPLETE cycle-of-fifths-descending ladder strictly inside the minor mode across nearly the whole album — unlike Vol. 1, which left the cycle at its track-7 major-lift; Vol. 2 stays on the descending cycle all the way down because there is no major excursion. Specifically: 1 ends on G (V of Cm, reread as Gm tonic of track 2). 2 ends on D (V of Gm, reread as Dm tonic of track 3). 3 ends on A (V of Dm, reread as Am tonic of track 4). 4 ends on E (V of Am, reread as Em tonic of track 5). 5 ends on B (V of Em, reread as Bm tonic of track 6). 6 ends on F# (V of Bm, reread as F#m tonic of track 7). 7 ends on a RINGING UNRESOLVED C# (V of F#m) that COLLAPSES — NOT lifted into A major as in Vol. 1, but reread DOWN as the C# MINOR tonic of track 8's aftermath (the deceptive collapse that is the album's no-payoff thesis at the 7->8 seam). 8 ends on G# (V of C#m, reread as G#m tonic of track 9). 9 ends on D# (V of G#m, reread enharmonically as Eb=D# the Ebm tonic of track 10). 10 ends on Bb (V of Ebm, reread as Bbm tonic of track 11). 11 ends on F (V of Bbm) RE-AIMED toward C-as-dominant — the ONE deliberate departure from the strict descending ladder, the harmony reaching for home to close the ring. 12 lands on the C-minor two-volume home tonic and closes COLD (no major resolution). The architectural rhyme is the cycle-of-fifths descending ladder run strictly inside minor — but where Vol. 1 broke the ladder at the 7->8 seam to lift into major, Vol. 2 keeps descending through that seam into minor and rides the cycle all the way down to its deepest centers before re-aiming home. The listener will not consciously hear most pivots — they will feel each transition as inevitable, and they will feel the 7->8 collapse (where Vol. 1 gave release) as the moment the brightness was promised and withheld.
album_notes.visual_continuity_notes: Twelve covers follow the album's narrative as a single descent through the empire's last season — the same 1987 deco-Miami / Vapor-Coast aesthetic and the same recurring chrome Ferrari Daytona subject as Vol. 1 (a real production car, evocative-generic, NOT a named character/object from any TV series or film), but the locked Vol. 1 cocaine-pink-and-turquoise pastel palette is deliberately CURDLED across this volume: progressively desaturated toward storm-grey, slate, bruised green and near-black, with the pink-and-turquoise neon surviving only as dim, dying, rain-smeared or submerged accents — the visual statement of the pastel dream souring. Aesthetic vocabulary otherwise locked: deco hotels and storefront neon (here dim/dead/flickering), palm silhouettes (here bending, whipped, downed), wet pavement and floodwater reflecting curdled signage, a heavy criminal-noir-AND-storm dread, no people visible, no faces, no text on any cover, all 1024x1024 SDXL-native with center-frame subject + foreground/background scene depth that survives reframing to 1344x768 wide and 768x1344 tall per the future-albums-native-aspect-gen memory. Track 1: the marina at heavy grey first light, a bruised storm sky massing, palms beginning to lean. Track 2: a causeway under a low overcast sky, dull chrome, dim neon. Track 3: the first storm light over the bay, palms bending, first rain. Track 4: a dim rain-streaked back office with an open ledger, neon bleeding through wet glass. Track 5: a deserted rain-soaked side street with an unmarked surveillance sedan in shadow. Track 6: a flooding street-grid from above in a hurricane outer band, palms whipped flat. Track 7: the eye wall hitting the coast, neon ripped loose and sparking, floodwater rising over the Daytona — the album's catastrophe-hero cover, the dark inverse of Vol. 1's bright hero covers. Track 8: an emptied flooded causeway in the rain with distant emergency lights. Track 9: a half-submerged flooded basement/garage with neon glowing under black floodwater. Track 10: a storm-wrecked shoreline, the Daytona half-buried at the tideline, grey sea to a horizonless grey sky. Track 11: a burnt-out waterline mansion smouldering at a grey dawn, one dying neon ember. Track 12: the same marina as track 1 (and as Vol. 1's track 12) at a colourless grey dawn with NO sunrise and NO warm light — the cold ring-close. The covers should read as one curdled-pastel storm-season descent and as the explicit visual sequel-and-inverse of Vol. 1 (same marina, same Daytona, same era — palette drained, weather turned, no warm light returning). Cover prompts specify center-frame subject + scene depth so native-aspect reframing to 1344x768 and 768x1344 preserves the composition without outpainting.
album_notes.execution_notes_for_pipeline: ACE-Step 1.5 turbo is the renderer of record (MusicGen license risk accepted per channel posture). 8 turbo inference steps means prompts front-load the most important descriptors: BPM + key/mode + signature voice (storm-grey DX7 FM electric-piano pad + curdled vice arp + haunted sax-flavored analog lead) + signature groove (soft sidechain on the pulsing arp) appear in the first half of every musicgen_prompt. Every musicgen_prompt explicitly ends 'instrumental, no vocals' to prevent ACE-Step from vocalizing. Per-track section markers in sections[] are formatted '[Section name - instrumentation notes]' as ACE-Step structural anchors per the engine's lyrics-marker convention. Per-track instrumentation notes specify synth voice (storm-grey DX7 pad vs FM-electric-piano arp vs sax-flavored analog lead vs analog sub bass), filter state (slow open / harsh open / flooded-wet / rolled-off), and rhythmic state (kick on every beat / kick on 1 only / drums dropped). Per-track durations sum to 3030 seconds (50.5 minutes) for the 12, matching Vol. 1's runtime, comfortably over the 45-minute album floor; the longest tracks carry the structural payloads (6 at 270s the storm-bridge, 7 at 300s the dark apex, 8 at 270s the no-payoff hinge, 12 at 300s the cold ring-close). All BPMs are within the 80-118 validation range (ceiling 112 at track 7, floor 86 at track 12). NO-PAYOFF GUARD FOR THE PIPELINE: this album is MINOR THROUGHOUT with NO major-key resolution at any point — the critical render-review checkpoints are the 7->8 seam (track 7 must collapse into C# MINOR, not lift into major; flagged in track 7's composer_notes), track 8 (must be exhausted minor aftermath, not relief), track 10's drowning sax (must drown in minor, not bloom), and track 12's coda (must end cold in C minor with no major chord, no fade-up tail, no stinger). If any render introduces a major-key lift at these points, re-cut darker. Per-album weather: route to US/Miami SE region (NWS), 4-city rotation Miami / Fort Lauderdale / Key West / Naples per the TWC-cities-must-match-album-theme memory — the SE-Florida storm-season weather directly fits Vol. 2's late-'87 hurricane-season dread (the storm-season anchor is a feature, not incidental). Cover prompts are SDXL-ready, all specify 1024x1024 and 'no people, no text'; generate at native aspect buckets (1024 square + 1344x768 + 768x1344) from scratch per the future-albums-native-aspect-gen memory (Vol. 1 was the legacy outpaint retrofit; Vol. 2 is native-gen). Sync-drop publish target: all 12 tracks render private and flip public together at the album's anchor timestamp per the sync-drop memory; mind the YT ~10/day channel rolling-24h cap and the GCP ~6/day video.insert project cap when scheduling — span the batch across two Pacific-midnight resets or use publish-staggered (6/day) and set the anchor accordingly. No per-track release-week language anywhere in the spec.
album_notes.danger_zone_compliance: danger_zone_keys for this album: ['miami_vice']. Hard rule applied per the album-title-danger-zone memory (the DOUBLE-HIT rule): NO title is BOTH (a) a named element of the Miami Vice TV show AND (b) a track on the Miami Vice OST (Jan Hammer 'Crockett's Theme', etc.). No character names anywhere (no 'Crockett', 'Tubbs', 'Castillo', 'Switek', 'Zito', 'Calderone', 'Lombard'). No soundtrack track titles (no 'Crockett's Theme', no named Jan Hammer cues, no named cues from any Miami Vice soundtrack album). Every Vol. 2 title was checked against both lists and clears: 'Season's Turn', 'Low Sky Cruise', 'Barometer Falling', 'The Books Won't Balance', 'The Wire Goes Live', 'Hurricane Bands', 'Eye Wall', 'Sirens in the Rain', 'Flooded Underworld', 'Washed Out to Sea', 'Empire of Ash', 'Cold Marina, Grey Dawn' — all evocative-generic, built from the approved storm-season + last-empire vocabulary (storm, barometer, hurricane, eye wall, flood, marina, ash, sirens), none a named show element AND a soundtrack track, none even a single-list hit on a character name. 'Daytona' (the recurring Ferrari 365 GTB/4 'Daytona' in the cover prompts) is a real production sports car and an evocative-generic object, single-hit-at-most, clears the double-hit rule (and appears only in prompts/notes, not as a title). The MUSICAL direction is Jan-Hammer-ADJACENT (pulsing FM arps, glassy DX7 electric-piano pads, sax-flavored leads, neon-noir cruise) and emphatically NOT a copy of any Miami Vice cue — every musicgen_prompt describes texture and feel in original terms and never references a named theme; the sax-noir lead motif is explicitly noted as 'NOT a literal sample of any soundtrack theme'.
album_notes.differentiation_from_prior_volumes: Miami Vice Vol. 2 is the explicit SEQUEL-AND-INVERSE of Vol. 1: same C-minor two-volume home, same 1987 Vapor-Coast aesthetic, same recurring chrome-Daytona subject, same three motifs (vice arp / sax-noir lead / palm-shadow pulse) — but every element CURDLED for the empire's last season. Where Vol. 1 was a nocturnal-cruise ring with a CENTERED DUAL-PEAK IN MAJOR (the only prior volume to break the all-minor posture, lifting into A-major Lydian at the chase's release, BPM 88-114, pastel-pink-and-turquoise surface over a criminal undercurrent), Vol. 2 is MINOR THROUGHOUT WITH NO MAJOR PAYOFF — the comedown Vol. 1's glamour earned: flat-Aeolian cruises instead of Vol. 1's Dorian brightness, Phrygian-from-track-4 and a Locrian-leaning-b5 apex instead of Vol. 1's single Phrygian peak, the apex COLLAPSING into minor instead of lifting into major, and a long five-track minor comedown carrying the structural weight instead of Vol. 1's dual-peak plateau. BPM band shifts down two ticks at both ends (86-112 vs 88-114), the last season slightly slower and heavier. The palette is Vol. 1's locked cocaine-pink-and-turquoise CURDLED toward storm-grey/slate/bruised-green, the pastel dying across the covers as the dream sours. Versus the rest of the catalog: Vol. 2 now aligns Miami Vice with the all-minor Tron Vol. 1 (Mobius ring, A-minor, PWM saws) and Neo-Tokyo Vol. 1 (vertical-descent ring, D-minor, analog brass) in refusing a major resolution — but keeps the distinct C-minor Miami home and the glassy DX7 FM-electric-piano + sax-flavored-lead voice signature it established in Vol. 1, and adds storm-season dread (thunder/wind/flood textures, hurricane-band and eye-wall imagery) as its own sonic and visual differentiator. The two Miami volumes read as one continuous fall on the same shelf: Vol. 1 is the glamour, Vol. 2 is the price — the same marina, the same car, the same coast, the brightness withdrawn, the season turned, no sun coming up at the end.
